title: Boeing, Israel's Technion to Develop Sustainable Aviation Fuel
content: Boeing and Israels Technion university said they were beginning to develop Sustainable Aviation Fuel from feedstocks including green hydrogen and carbon dioxide to enable the aviation sectors long term growth, noting that SAF, made largely from waste or used cooking oil, can cut emissions significantly compared with traditional jet fuel but remains two to five times more expensive. They said that after completing an initial feasibility phase, development was set to begin and move toward competitive commercial production, supported by Boeings commitment to deliver commercial airplanes capable of flying 100 percent on SAF by 2030 and the industrys goal of zero net emissions by 2050. However, the International Air Transport Association forecast SAF would account for only 0.7 percent of total jet fuel in 2025 and the 2050 target could be missed. Boeing added that developing SAF on a large scale could take a few years, while also announcing a cybersecurity research centre with Israels Ben Gurion University to support next generation aviation and aerospace systems.
author: Analysts Account
